{"plan": "```json
{
  "steps": [
    "1. Initialize the project with npm init and install dependencies (express, supertest, etc.).",
    "2. Create the project structure with src/ and modules/.",
    "3. Implement src/app.js to export an Express app and set up routes.",
    "4. Implement src/server.js to start the server without calling listen().",
    "5. Create src/lib/ with errors.js, id.js, and store.js.",
    "6. Implement src/modules/projects/ with routes.js and service.js.",
    "7. Implement src/modules/tasks/ with routes.js and service.js.",
    "8. Implement src/modules/members/ with routes.js and service.js.",
    "9. Implement src/modules/comments/ with routes.js and service.js.",
    "10. Implement the health check endpoint.",
    "11. Implement the project endpoints (GET /projects, POST /projects, GET /projects/:id).",
    "12. Implement the members endpoints (POST /projects/:id/members, GET /projects/:id/members)."
  ],
  "files": {
    "src/app.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst router = express.Router();\n\n// Import the store\nconst store = require('./lib/store');\n\n// Import the routes\nconst projectsRouter = require('./modules/projects/routes');\nconst tasksRouter = require('./modules/tasks/routes');\nconst membersRouter = require('./modules/members/routes');\nconst commentsRouter = require('./modules/comments/routes');\n\n// Use the routes\nrouter.use('/projects', projectsRouter);\nrouter.use('/projects/:id/tasks', tasksRouter);\nrouter.use('/projects/:id/members', membersRouter);\nrouter.use('/projects/:id/tasks/:id/comments', commentsRouter);\n\n// Export the router\nmodule.exports = router;",
    "src/server.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst app = require('./app');\n\nconst server = app();\n\n// Start the server\nconst PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;\nserver.listen(PORT, () => {\n  console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`);\n});\n\n// Export the server for testing\nmodule.exports = server;",
    "src/lib/errors.js": "class AppError extends Error {\n  constructor(message, statusCode) {\n    super(message);\n    this.statusCode = statusCode;\n  }\n}\n\nmodule.exports = { AppError };",
    "src/lib/id.js": "const generateId = () => {\n  return crypto.randomUUID();\n};\n\nmodule.exports = { generateId };",
    "src/lib/store.js": "const { AppError } = require('./errors');\nconst { generateId } = require('./id');\n\nlet projects = {};\nlet tasks = {};\nlet members = {};\n\n// Projects\nconst setProject = (project) => {\n  projects[project.id] = project;\n};\n\nconst getProject = (id) => {\n  if (!projects[id]) {\n    throw new AppError('Project not found', 404);\n  }\n  return projects[id];\n};\n\n// Tasks\nconst setTask = (projectId, task) => {\n  if (!projects[projectId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Project not found', 404);\n  }\n  if (!tasks[projectId]) {\n    tasks[projectId] = {};\n  }\n  tasks[projectId][task.id] = task;\n};\n\nconst getTask = (projectId, taskId) => {\n  if (!projects[projectId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Project not found', 404);\n  }\n  if (!tasks[projectId] || !tasks[projectId][taskId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Task not found', 404);\n  }\n  return tasks[projectId][taskId];\n};\n\n// Members\nconst setMember = (projectId, member) => {\n  if (!projects[projectId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Project not found', 404);\n  }\n  if (!members[projectId]) {\n    members[projectId] = {};\n  }\n  members[projectId][member.id] = member;\n};\n\nconst getMember = (projectId, memberId) => {\n  if (!projects[projectId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Project not found', 404);\n  }\n  if (!members[projectId] || !members[projectId][memberId]) {\n    throw new AppError('Member not found', 404);\n  }\n  return members[projectId][memberId];\n};\n\n// Export the store functions\nmodule.exports = {\n  projects,\n  tasks,\n  members,\n  setProject,\n  getProject,\n  setTask,\n  getTask,\n  setMember,\n  getMember\n};",
    "src/modules/projects/routes.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst router = express.Router();\n\nconst { setProject, getProject } = require('./lib/store');\n\n// Create a new project\nrouter.post('/', (req, res) => {\n  const project = {\n    id: req.body.id || generateId(),\n    name: req.body.name,\n    description: req.body.description,\n  };\n  setProject(project);\n  res.status(201).json(project);\n});\n\n// Get all projects\nrouter.get('/', (req, res) => {\n  res.json(Object.values(projects));\n});\n\n// Get a single project\nrouter.get('/:id', (req, res) => {\n  const project = getProject(req.params.id);\n  res.json(project);\n});\n\nmodule.exports = router;",
    "src/modules/projects/service.js": "const { setProject, getProject } = require('./lib/store');\n\n// This is the service layer for projects\n// We can add more complex logic here if needed\nmodule.exports = {\n  // No additional services for now\n};",
    "src/modules/tasks/routes.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst router = express.Router();\n\nconst { getProject, setTask, getTask } = require('./lib/store');\n\n// Create a task for a project\nrouter.post('/:projectId/tasks', (req, res) => {\n  const task = {\n    id: req.body.id || generateId(),\n    title: req.body.title,\n    description: req.body.description,\n  };\n  setTask(req.params.projectId, task);\n  res.status(201).json(task);\n});\n\n// Get tasks for a project\nrouter.get('/:projectId/tasks', (req, res) => {\n  const tasks = req.params.projectId ? Object.values(tasks[req.params.projectId] || {}).map(task => ({\n    ...task,\n    projectId: req.params.projectId\n  })) : [];\n  res.json(tasks);\n});\n\n// Get a single task\nrouter.get('/:projectId/tasks/:taskId', (req, res) => {\n  const task = getTask(req.params.projectId, req.params.taskId);\n  res.json(task);\n});\n\nmodule.exports = router;",
    "src/modules/members/routes.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst router = express.Router();\n\nconst { getProject, setMember } = require('./lib/store');\n\n// Add a member to a project\nrouter.post('/:projectId/members', (req, res) => {\n  const member = {\n    id: req.body.id || generateId(),\n    name: req.body.name,\n    email: req.body.email,\n  };\n  setMember(req.params.projectId, member);\n  res.status(201).json(member);\n});\n\n// Get members of a project\nrouter.get('/:projectId/members', (req, res) => {\n  const members = req.params.projectId ? Object.values(members[req.params.projectId] || {}).map(member => ({\n    ...member,\n    projectId: req.params.projectId\n  })) : [];\n  res.json(members);\n});\n\nmodule.exports = router;",
    "src/modules/comments/routes.js": "const express = require('express');\nconst router = express.Router();\n\nconst { getTask } = require('./lib/store');\n\n// Create a comment for a task\nrouter.post('/:taskId/comments', (req, res) => {\n  // This is a placeholder for the comment creation\n  // We would need to implement the store for comments\n  res.status(201).json({\n    id: generateId(),\n    text: req.body.text\n  });\n});\n\nmodule.exports = router;"
  ],
  "checklist": [
    "1. Verify all dependencies are installed.",
    "2. Ensure the store.js handles all CRUD operations.",
    "3. Test the routes with Postman or similar.",
    "4. Make sure the server.js exports the server for testing.",
    "5. Implement error handling consistently.",
    "6. Add comments for clarity where necessary.",
    "7. Ensure the code follows RESTful conventions.",
    "8. Test the health check endpoint.",
    "9. Verify the project endpoints work.",
    "10. Verify the task and member endpoints work.",
    "1" }
message.thinking:
We are building a Node.js monolith for a Project Management API that must pass Oracle tests (using Supertest).
 The project must be structured with modules in `src/modules/` and have at least 12 source files under `src/`.
 We must avoid using `uuid` and use `node:crypto.randomUUID()` for generating IDs.
 We must have `src/app.js` (app export) and `src/server.js` (entry point) without calling `listen()` on import.
 We must use consistent error responses and avoid uncaught exceptions.

 Steps to follow (max 12 steps):

 1. Set up the project with necessary files and folders.
 2. Install required dependencies (express, supertest, etc.).
 3. Create the basic structure with `src/` and modules.
 4. Implement the `src/app.js` to export an app function and set up routes.
 5. Implement the `src/server.js` to start the server.
 6. Create the `src/lib/` directory with `errors.js` and `id.js`.
 7. Implement the services and routes for each module (projects, tasks, members, comments).
 8. Ensure in-memory storage for each entity (projects, tasks, members, comments) and handle interactions.
 9. Implement the health check endpoint.
10. Implement the project endpoints (GET /projects, POST /projects, GET /projects/:id).
11. Implement the members endpoints (POST /projects/:id/members, GET /projects/:id/members).
12. Implement the tasks and comments endpoints (POST /projects/:id/tasks, GET /projects/:id/tasks, PATCH /projects/:id/tasks/:id, POST /projects/:id/tasks/:id/comments, GET /projects/:id/tasks/:id/comments).
13. Write the Oracle tests (using Supertest) and ensure they pass.

 However, note that the Oracle tests are provided and must be passed.

 Let's break down the implementation:

 We'll use Express.

 Important: 
   - Use `node:crypto.randomUUID()` for generating IDs.
   - Use consistent error responses.

 We'll structure the code as follows:

   src/
     app.js
     server.js
     lib/
        errors.js
        id.js
     modules/
        projects/
            routes.js
            service.js
        tasks/
            routes.js
            service.js
        members/
            routes.js
            service.js
        comments/
            routes.js
            service.js

 We must have at least 12 source files. We can break down the services and routes into multiple files.

 Let's plan the files:

   src/app.js
   src/server.js
   src/lib/errors.js
   src/lib/id.js
   src/modules/projects/routes.js
   src/modules/projects/service.js
   src/modules/tasks/routes.js
   src/modules/tasks/service.js
   src/modules/members/routes.js
   src/modules/members/service.js
   src/modules/comments/routes.js
   src/modules/comments/service.js

 That's 12 files.

 Now, let's write the plan in JSON format as required.

 Note: The Oracle tests are provided and must be passed. We must ensure that the code meets the test expectations.

 We'll use Express and set up the app in `src/app.js` to export an Express app.

 We'll use in-memory storage for projects, tasks, members, and comments. However, note that the members are associated with projects, so we need to manage that.

 We'll create a shared repository (in-memory) for projects, tasks, and members. But note: the test failure context says that members service should reuse shared projects repository. We'll have:

   - A shared in-memory store for projects (by id, name, etc.)
   - A shared in-memory store for tasks (by project id and task id)
   - A shared in-memory store for members (by project id and member id)

 However, note that the test expects that when we try to add a member to a project, the project must exist. So we need to check the project exists when adding a member.

 We'll structure the services to use the shared in-memory stores.

 Let's define the in-memory stores in `src/lib/id.js` and `src/lib/errors.js`? Actually, we can define the stores in the service files or in a separate store module. But to keep it simple, we'll define them in the service files and pass the store as a dependency.

 However, the requirement says that members service should reuse shared projects repository. So we can have a shared store for projects, tasks, and members. We can create a `store.js` that holds all the in-memory data and then the services can use that.

 But note: the test failure context says that members service should reuse shared projects repository, meaning that the same store for projects is used by the members service. So we can have a single store that holds:

   - projects: { [id]: Project }
   - tasks: { [projectId]: { [taskId]: Task } }
   - members: { [projectId]: { [memberId]: Member } }

 We can create a `store` object in `src/lib/store.js` and then export it. Then each service can use the store.

 However, the test does not require a separate store for each service, so we'll have a single store.

 Let's create:

   src/lib/store.js

 But note: the requirement says at least 12 files. We can break down the store into separate files for each entity? Or we can keep it as one file.

 Alternatively, we can avoid a separate store file and define the stores in the service files, but then we have to share the same store instance.

 We'll create a `store` module that exports an object with methods to get and set projects, tasks, and members.

 However, the Oracle tests are provided and must be passed. We must be cautious.

 Let's stick to the following structure:

   src/
     app.js
     server.js
     lib/
        errors.js
        id.js
        store.js
        errors.js (for error handling)
        id.js (for generating IDs)
        store.js (for in-memory storage)

 But wait, we have 12 files. We can break down the store into separate files for each entity? Or we can keep it as one.

 Alternatively, we can have:

   src/modules/projects/store.js
   src/modules/tasks/store.js
   src/modules/members/store.js
   src/modules/comments/store.js

 But that would be 4 more files and we are already at 8 (app, server, lib/errors, lib/id, lib/store, and then 4 per module). That would be 14 files.

 We are allowed to have more than 12, but the requirement says at least 12. We can break down the store if needed.

 However, to keep the code clean and avoid circular dependencies, we can have a shared store.

 Let's do:

   src/lib/store.js

 This store will have:

   - projects: Map or object for projects
   - tasks: object where keys are project IDs and values are another object (Map or object) of task IDs to tasks
   - members: object where keys are project IDs and values are another object (Map or object) of member IDs to members

 We'll use a simple object for each, but we can use Map for better performance if needed.

 But note: the test expects that when we try to get a project that doesn't exist, we get 404.

 We'll implement the store with functions to get and set.

 However, the requirement says that the members service should reuse the shared projects repository. So we'll have one store for everything.

 Let's create `src/lib/store.js` and define:

   let projects = {};
   let tasks = {};
   let members = {};

   Then functions to set and get.

 But note: the test expects that when we try to add a member to a project, the project must exist. So we can check the project in the store.

 We'll implement the store with:

   - setProject(project)
   - getProject(id)
   - setTask(projectId, task)
   - getTask(projectId, taskId)
   - setMember(projectId, member)
   - getMember(projectId, memberId)

 We'll use the store in each service.

 Now, let's write the plan in JSON format.

 We are limited to 12 steps, so we'll list the steps and then the file contents.

   - A bullet list of steps (max 12)
   - A list of files and what they contain
   - Checkpoints (build/test)

        "Step 1: ...",
        ...
     ],

        "src/app.js": "...",
        ...
     },

        "Build the project and run tests",
        ...
     ]
   }

 But note: the problem says to output in a specific format.

   - A bullet list of steps (max 12) in the format of lines starting with "- "
   - A list of files and what they contain
   - Checkpoints (build/test)

 We'll do:

 Let's start with `src/app.js`:

   We'll create an Express app, use the store, and set up the routes.
